questions.q: What is the difference between int* p and int& r in C++?
options: p is a pointer (nullable), r is a reference (cannot be null or reassigned)
p is a reference, r is a pointer
Both are identical in behavior
p can be reassigned, r is immutable
questions.explanation: Pointers can be null and reassigned; references are aliases that must be bound at declaration and cannot change targets. Option 1 reverses the definitions. Option 2 misses that references cannot be null.

questions.q: What does template<typename T> void foo(T x) declare?
options: A function template that works with any type T
A function that only accepts pointers
A macro expansion
A class template
questions.explanation: Template functions are instantiated at compile-time for concrete types. foo(5) creates foo<int>, foo('a') creates foo<char>. Option 1 confuses templates with generic pointers. Option 2 misses template-specific syntax.

options: Resources acquired in constructors are released in destructors
All resources must be freed manually
Automatic garbage collection like Java
A design pattern for multithreading
questions.explanation: RAII ties resource lifetime to object scope. Constructors acquire (open file, allocate memory), destructors release. This avoids leaks even on exceptions. Option 1 requires manual management. Option 2 misses the automatic destructors.

questions.q: What does const int x = 5 mean?
options: x is an immutable integer constant
x is a pointer to a constant integer
x can be modified with const_cast
x is mutable if reinterpreted
questions.explanation: const before the type makes the variable immutable. Attempting x = 6 is a compile error. Option 1 confuses const with pointer declarations. Option 2 is possible but violates const semantics.

questions.q: What does int* const p allow?
options: p itself cannot be reassigned, but *p can be modified
p can be reassigned, but *p cannot be modified
Neither p nor *p can be modified
The declaration is invalid
questions.explanation: const after * makes the pointer immutable. The pointed-to value is mutable. Reading right-to-left: p is a const pointer to int. Option 1 reverses mutability. Option 2 requires const int* p.

questions.q: What does class A { operator int() const {} } enable?
options: Implicit conversion of A to int
Explicit conversion with a cast
Overloading the int constructor
Defining a static conversion function
questions.explanation: Operator overloading defines conversions. operator int() allows A obj; int x = obj (implicit). Explicit conversion requires explicit operator int(). Option 1 requires using the keyword explicit.

options: A vector with 5 elements, all initialized to 3
A vector with 5 capacity, 3 elements
A vector of size 3 containing [5]
Invalid syntax
questions.explanation: Vector constructor v(count, value) creates count copies of value. v(5, 3) = [3, 3, 3, 3, 3]. Option 1 confuses count with capacity. Option 2 reverses the argument order.

questions.q: What does template<typename T> using vec = std::vector<T> define?
options: A template alias for std::vector
A template function
A template class inheriting from vector
A template specialization
questions.explanation: Template aliases (using) simplify syntax. vec<int> is equivalent to std::vector<int>. Option 1 confuses aliases with function templates. Option 2 is a separate feature.

questions.q: What happens with int x = 5; int& r = x; r = 10?
options: x becomes 10 because r is an alias to x
r is assigned 10 but x remains 5
Undefined behavior
Syntax error
questions.explanation: References are aliases. Modifying r modifies the underlying x. r and x refer to the same memory. Option 1 treats r as a copy. Option 2 is incorrect—references don't create scope boundaries.

questions.q: What does volatile int x indicate?
options: x may be modified externally; the compiler must reread it each time
x is a constant that cannot be modified
x will be garbage-collected
x is thread-safe
questions.explanation: volatile tells the compiler not to optimize away reads/writes—useful for hardware registers or shared memory. Compiler rereads x each access. Option 1 is incorrect—volatile is not about constants. Option 2 lacks synchronization guarantees.
